segments.text: Uh, warm welcomes to you all.
Uh, thank you very much, uh, coming along, uh, this morning.
Welcome, John. Um, it just says a, a little bit of a, a, a,
a start to this conversation
that clearly we've, uh, got a couple of reports.
One is importantly your one,
but also we have, uh, Tata report with us, uh, as well.
And we are as a committee, 50 K
with regards to discussion.
But, uh, a very good read, take it as read, uh,
and you've got your, your recommendations in John, I'll,
I'll leave it to you how you wish to handle, uh,
presenting the report you've provided to us.
Sokoto. Thank you for asking me along.
Do you know Lindsey well? Uh, Andrew McConnell,
deputy Auditor General, uh, also an officer of Parliament.
So, uh, we, we are here today
and a team of people including Dan n whose team, uh,
produced the report, uh, and, and the team's there.
So we've got everyone in and, good.
I didn't realize we had that many ties in the office.
So, um, uh,
there's obviously the tire supplies being dropped, uh,
for this particular meeting.
I, I would like to say a few things.
I know you've had a chance to read it,
but I just feel like I want
to just express some things in this report that really sure.
Affected me as well. Um, uh,
I think, uh, and I, we've done quite a lot of media,
and this is quite interesting actually,
that there has been such media interest in this.
But I would say this is one of the most important reports
of my term, um, seven years in my term.
Uh, I think for lots of reasons.
Firstly, the accountability, uh, of public
agencies is at the base of this report.
Um, this is a legal compliance issue if you just
take everything else away from it.
Fundamentally, this is the law
and we should be complying with it as public agencies.
There's also a lot of money, uh, 2.7 billion,
and a lot of risk about settlements.
Uh, and taking all that aside,
there's also the constitutional significance
of the treaty settlement process
and the implementation of settlements that have been made.
So for lots of reasons, it's important.
And also when we saw what we saw, um, you know,
it, it just, uh,
you feel like there things need to be done here.
Uh, so I just want to say that the Auditor General
is normally a cold-hearted accountant,
but when I read this, I thought, um, uh, I felt like, uh,
we needed to act on this.
Um, I think the, uh,
the other thing that you would be very conscious of, um,
you know, the treaty settlement process, um, fee
of settlement legislation, uh, historical account apology,
redress, various forms of redress.
But the expectation
that this is about resetting relationships, uh,
I think was really one of the other big things
that came through for me.
Uh, I've been tangentially involved in treaty settlements
for some time as a public servant,
but actually that idea of resetting relationships that was,
you know, cabinet expectations around this,
which have been endured between multiple governments, uh,
what we looked at was eight agencies.
There are about 70% of the commitments that have been made
through treasury settlements, um, uh, including central
and local government agencies
and three particular settlements.
We looked at the, the lens through that,
but we talked to a lot of agencies.
And so the issues in this report,
I think we can say are widespread.
Um, I thought I'd just do two things.
One, just quickly summarize some of the findings,
and then perhaps just a little bit about my
reflections on those findings.
And then, uh, a little bit about call to action.
So if you can bear with me for a few minutes, five minutes,
maybe, uh, what we found, well, there were some good,
there was some good practice we found.
Um, so it's, uh, it's good to be able to say that.
But over the time
that treaty settlements have been in place,
which is now 30 years,
and some of these are 10 years old, right?
These are not done yesterday,
agencies hadn't really got their heads around
what the commitments were,
the complexity of those commitments.
Uh, they need to work across agency sometimes
to deliver on those commitments,
and then, of course, to actually deliver the redress.
So at the basic level, there wasn't enough understanding.
Mm-hmm. The rights of first refusal, we call out,
particularly because that's a major part of redress and,
and, and settling, uh, settling, uh,
and the fact that those systems memorials weren't
necessarily well managed or in place in all cases.
And some properties have been sold
that should have had a right at first refusal when they
found, when Lynn's looked at a number of agencies, a number
of, uh, memorials that needed to be put in place.
I think the weakness of oversight is another real issue.
Um, the support for es, the expectation on es,
the reporting to ministers themselves
and to parliament about progress of seafood
and just old fashioned accountability for non-delivery
that we would expect in legal commitments.
There's already been tens of millions, uh,
paid in compensation for, um, redress
that couldn't be delivered as intended.
And there have been some improvements.
Tara put in place, Hey, UA
and teta, which is the, uh, portal.
So it's not that there hasn't been some action
and attention to this,
but we also point out in the report, they, uh,
there's more work to be done in both those areas.
Uh, particularly one in five commitments are not updated
because they're outside core government.
They're in, um, SOEs, crown Reserve, et cetera.
Uh, if I was to step back a little bit
and talk about my reaction, uh, to this, uh, a few things,
um, if this was tax compliance,
imagine that, you know,
so why is it that tax compliance is really treated this
seriously, but this is equally legislative compliance just
as important, probably more important in many ways, um,
in terms of constitutional significance,
although I don't wanna say tax compliance isn't
important as auditor general.
It's very important. I think the other thing, second
reaction I might have had is this concept of settlement.
And I think when you come at it in a sense, maybe,
and I can talk about that, you kind of think
of a property transaction.
'cause that's where you hear the word settlement,
where you do the business
and then you both go your separate ways.
That's not what these are. These are settlements of wrongs.
They're resetting a relationship.
And the crown, I think, has seen this
after the treaty settlement process as a series
of transactions that needed
to be managed rather than a relationship
that needed to be reset.
These are meant to be durable.
These are called settlements for a reason. Right?
And I think what we've seen here is there is a risk,
and Tara ti has made that point itself.
There's a risk to the durability of settlements
and the goodwill that is built through many, many years
of moving towards a settlement process.
There's a risk to durability.
And that goodwill that's built
through the settlement process by not implementing the
settlements as expected was required.
And my other reaction was just this
tolerance to non-delivery.
Firstly, the lack of visibility of non-delivery,
and then whether explicit
or implicit a tolerance for not delivering.
And that, you know, we can't expect
that this is a legal compliance at the, at a space.
Right? And I think if I was to step back
and think about my children, um, uh,
that this could be a massive opportunity,
but we risk losing it.
We risk losing it by not implementing these settlements,
both in terms of the actual listed redress,
that's clear enough in the settlement process,
but in the reset relationships that comes with.
And so there's a massive opportunity for the country here
that these, this report, I think, points to being risk.
Um, we make some call to action, uh, in here, um,
some recommendations, obviously.
But if I was to, to think about this committee, um,
and to take it a bit out of my emotional response to my, um,
auditor, general cold-hearted response, um, uh,
parliament being fully updated with this.
Now, I know there's a, um, on a report on, uh, on progress,
but as we point out in the report, sometimes
you might see one commitment that hasn't been met,
but that could be the fundamental essence of a settlement,
and it might just count as one.
So, you know, understanding just how complex
and difficult settlements can be.
Talking to the post settlement governance entities,
I think is really, you know,
we've talked through this process.
Um, I think that from what I can gather,
they've read this report
and see that it reflects their concerns.
Every agency had some issue in dealing with a settlement,
implementing a settlement.
Every PSGE has had some sort of issue as well.

And, uh, land information.
New Zealand clearly has got a big role here

memorials, uh,
and then TPK, which of course is now transitioning, uh,

So whether this committee wants to write us
by the committees, pointing out some
of the things you would like them to,
to be focused on as well.
Hmm. So that was kind of my, uh,
factual summary and my emotional reaction to this.
Um, I think it is, as I said at the start, um, up there
with the most important, uh, reports in my team.
Um, look forward to a conversation with you about that.
Thank you very much, John.
And if I may just start, uh, the conversation, uh, I suppose

you could point to many factors,
and you've pointed to a lot of that there.

This is a, a, a pan, uh, term
pan period of time.

to achieve the success that we're aiming for?
So most of this work was looking at a period
before the last budget and the cycle and the economic cycle.
And now, so this is looking at a time, uh,
before that mostly, but it looks now.
Uh, so that's the first point. The second point I would make
on that is coming back to my core here,
these are legal commitments
and they should be a priority for agencies.
And that, I think means more than just simple resourcing.
It means good governance. It means good reporting.
It means transparency when things have gone along,
it means acting on those issues
and trying to deal with it quickly.
So I think if you were looking at an agency
and its prioritization is really the question.
Mm-hmm. And I would've said, this should be a core priority.
And we see things like, um, uh, crown forest lease land,
which they were given five years to,
and I think we mentioned 23,
that this the five year, wouldn't it?
And that's 'cause they started late.
They thought it was simpler than it was. Right.

it's resource seems a separate question to my mind.

to drive the right incentives.

be incentivized to deliver that.

What level of transparency do US

Anything.

source_metadata.description:
14 May 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee
Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlements
Controller and Auditor-General

John Ryan, Auditor-General
Andrew McConnell, Deputy Auditor-General
Leeanne McAviney, Assistant Auditor-General, Sector Performance
Evaan Aramakutu, Manager, Sector Performance
Ethan McKenzie, Senior Performance Auditor
Lyndsey Gibson, Sector Manager
